Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is now established as the preferred imaging modality for the detection and evaluation of active inflammatory lesions in the spine and sacroiliac joints (SIJ) of patients with spondyloarthritis (SpA). MRI of active inflammatory lesions is of value not only for diagnostic purposes but also for the assessment of therapeutic agents that alleviate inflammation in SpA. In particular, the administration of anti-tumor necrosis factor–α (anti-TNF–α) therapies has been shown to lead to rapid amelioration of active inflammatory lesions on MRI in patients with SpA\(^{1,2}\). This has led to the development of scoring methodologies for active inflammatory lesions in the spine and SIJ that are principally directed at the clinical trial evaluation of new therapies but may also be used in observational studies.

Two scoring methodologies have been described for evaluation of active inflammatory lesions in the spine. Both are based on assessment of a discovertebral unit (DVU), which represents the region between 2 imaginary lines drawn through the middle of 2 adjacent vertebrae. The first method, the ASspiMRI-a (AS spinal MRI activity) index, scores the severity of bone edema and erosions...

at each DVU according to a scoring scheme of 0 to 6, with higher values assigned to the presence of erosions\textsuperscript{3}. The score for edema is based on the total area involved in the DVU according to a < 25%, 25%–50%, and > 50% grading scheme. The range of scores is 0–138. An adaptation of this method, the Berlin method\textsuperscript{4}, does not score erosions but otherwise is the same as ASspiMRI-a, and its scoring range is 0–69. For both methods all 23 DVU in the spine are scored in a single sagittal plane of view. The ASspiMRI-a method has been shown to be reproducible and to discriminate between treatment groups in trials of anti–TNF therapies\textsuperscript{3}. However, reliability of the ASspiMRI-a method was rather poor in a multireader exercise, although the Berlin method was better\textsuperscript{4}. The second method, the Spondyloarthritis Research Consortium of Canada (SPARCC) MRI spinal inflammation index, evaluates the extent of the acute lesion in 3 dimensions, which is appropriate because inflammatory lesions are often asymmetrical\textsuperscript{5}. This method has been shown to have excellent reproducibility and to be highly discriminatory between anti–TNF–α treatments and placebo so that only small numbers of patients (20–30) are required for proof-of-concept studies\textsuperscript{1}. Moreover, a multireader exercise confirmed that this method demonstrated the most consistent reproducibility between different reader pairs, which included readers with no prior experience with application of this method\textsuperscript{4}. Although additional methods for scoring acute lesions have been cited in the literature, none has been described in adequate detail or sufficiently validated to enable widespread use.

As for the spine, the SPARCC SIJ scoring method evaluates acute lesions in 3 dimensions and has undergone validation for both reproducibility and discrimination\textsuperscript{1,6}. In addition to being highly discriminatory in placebo-controlled trials of anti–TNF–α therapy, it has been shown to be consistently reproducible in a multireader exercise\textsuperscript{7}.

An essential prerequisite for more widespread implementation of any outcome measure is development of a validated mechanism for knowledge translation. Where expertise for a particular outcome measure is limited, direct and widespread knowledge transfer using traditional formats such as seminars is not feasible. Web-based learning has been implemented in various fields and has the potential to reach a wide audience but has rarely been the subject of formal validation. We describe the development and validation of Web-based training modules for the quantitative evaluation of acute lesions in the spine and SIJ according to the SPARCC method.

**METHODS**

*Development of a Web-based training module for the quantitative assessment of acute lesions in the SIJ.* The SIJ training module was developed by 2 radiologists and a rheumatologist, who are the original developers of the SPARCC SIJ scoring method for acute lesions. The module focuses on evaluation of SIJ in the semicoronal orientation as described in the SPARCC method along the following steps:

1. A description of the normal anatomy of the SIJ as visualized on both short-tau inversion recovery (STIR) and T1-weighted sequences. A point of emphasis is that MRI scans are difficult to reproduce with identical orientation of the joints, and landmarks are variable.

2. Basic imaging requirements. In particular, the simultaneous availability of T1-weighted sequences greatly enhances the interpretation of SIJ anatomy.

3. Defining the rules for dividing each SIJ into quadrants, which constitutes the basic unit for the quantification of active inflammatory lesions (Figure 1). It is recommended that SIJ semicoronal images are read in a consistent manner from anterior to posterior, with the first anterior slice being the slice where there is at least 1 cm of vertical height in at least one SIJ (Figure 2). A visible joint of less than 3 cm vertical height is defined as having only upper sacral and upper iliac quadrants. A visible joint of 3 cm or more of vertical height is defined as having 4 quadrants divided at the midpoint into equal upper and lower sacral and iliac quadrants (Figure 3). At the posterior aspect of the SIJ there is a natural division of the joint into upper and lower quadrants by intervening fat and fibrous tissue (Figure 4). When less than 1 cm of a quadrant is visible, it is no longer scored (Figure 5).

4. Defining the reference for scoring of the abnormal increase in bone marrow signal on the STIR sequence that indicates an acute lesion. The primary reference for normal bone marrow signal intensity is the center of the sacrum, at the same craniocaudal level as the bone marrow being assessed. This site is most likely to be normal and is less subject to fatty change or inflammation.

5. The approach to scoring bone marrow edema. Each quadrant is scored dichotomously for the presence of bone edema (present/absent). For each semicoronal slice the scoring range is therefore 0–8. Depth and intensity of bone edema is scored for each joint as a whole so that the scoring range per semicoronal slice is 0–2 for each feature. The maximum score per semicoronal slice is therefore 0–12. Scores are entered into a customized Web-based software scoring program that displays a diagram of the joint quadrants in a semicoronal slice (Figure 6). Brightness of “intense bone marrow edema” is defined as being comparable to, or greater than, the appearance of blood in presacral veins. “Depth” of bone marrow edema is defined as positive when 1 cm or more of continuous increase in STIR signal extends in a horizontal direction away from the joint space. The SPARCC SIJ score was originally developed to score acute lesions in 6 consecutive semicoronal slices since, in general, this number of slices...

6. Technical considerations aimed at optimizing display, enhancing viewing conditions, detection, and recording of lesions.

**Development of Web-based training module for quantitative assessment of acute lesions in the spine.** The spine training module was developed by a rheumatologist and a radiologist, who are the original developers of the SPARCC spine scoring method for acute lesions. The module focuses on evaluation of the spine in the sagittal orientation as described in the SPARCC method in the following steps:

1. A description of the normal anatomy of the spine as visualized on both short STIR and T1-weighted sagittal sequences. A point of emphasis is that the anatomy of the posterior and lateral spine is more complex due to overlapping structures.

2. Basic imaging requirements. In particular, the simultaneous availability of T1-weighted sequences greatly enhances the interpretation of spinal anatomy.

3. Defining the reference for scoring abnormal increase in bone marrow signal on the STIR sequence that indicates an acute lesion. The primary reference for normal bone marrow signal intensity is the center of the vertebral body (Figure 7).

4. Defining the basic unit for scoring acute lesions at each spinal level. This is the discovertebral unit, which is defined by the region between 2 imaginary lines through the midpoint of adjacent vertebrae and includes the disc, vertebral endplates, and adjacent bone marrow (Figure 8).

5. The approach to scoring bone marrow edema on sagittal slices of the spine. DVU is divided into quadrants and each quadrant is scored dichotomously for the presence of bone edema (present/absent). This is repeated in 3 consecutive sagittal slices, giving a maximum score of 12. Depth and intensity of bone edema is scored for each DVU as a whole so that the maximum additional score per DVU for 3 consecutive sagittal slices is 3 for each feature, and the total maximum score per DVU is then 18 (Figure 9). Scores are entered into a diagrammatic representation of the DVU in 3 consecutive sagittal slices (Figure 10). Brightness of “intense bone marrow edema” is defined as comparable to or greater than the appearance of cerebrospinal fluid. “Depth” of bone marrow edema is defined as positive when 1 cm or more of continuous increase in STIR signal extends in a vertical direction away from the vertebral endplate.

6. Selection of the 6 most severely affected DVU if the scoring system is recommended for evaluation of therapeutic interventions in clinical trials. This follows a global evaluation of the entire spine for inflammatory lesions on both pre- and post-treatment images because the lesion may no longer be evident at one timepoint if the treatment is very effective. All 23 DVU may also be scored for acute lesions, and this may be preferable for longitudinal

studies, but this may force the scoring of regions of artefact and is clearly more time-consuming.

Both training modules can be accessed at www.arthritisdoctor.ca.

**Validation of training modules.** Validation was conducted using MRI scans from 32 patients with AS fulfilling the modified New York criteria\textsuperscript{8} who were recruited to a randomized (1:1) placebo-controlled trial of anti–TNF–α therapy. MRI scanning was conducted at baseline and 12 weeks. All scans were reviewed on work stations with large screens and image manipulation software ideally suited for this trial (Merge Healthcare eFilm, Milwaukee, WI, USA). This system permitted simultaneous display of all 8 MRI sequences (T1 and STIR for upper and lower spine for both timepoints) at original (life-size) dimensions, and this could be repeated for the semicoronal images of the SIJ. Readers had full-windowing capability and could choose to score the timepoints in any order. Scores were recorded electronically using a custom-designed Web-based computer-assisted masked reading program; the reader was able to see all scores and all scans simultaneously before committing to the final score. A unique MRI study number was allocated to each of the 32 patients, allocation being done by a technologist unconnected with the study using computer-generated random numbers. Each scan was rated by 3 independent readers, who were rheumatology fellows from Denmark, Thailand, and Mexico, and who were blinded to patients’ identities, treatments, and imaging timepoints. One reader (SJP) had some prior experience in reading and interpreting MRI, but the other 2 readers had minimal prior training in MRI. Readers conducted their first readings of these scans after receiving basic training in the principles of MRI and then reading the original manuscripts describing the SPARCC MRI spine and SIJ scoring systems. One month later, readers reviewed the online training modules and then repeated their readings of the MRI scans.

**Magnetic resonance imaging.** All MRI scans of the spine were performed with 1.5 Tesla systems (Siemens, Erlangen, Germany) using appropriate surface coils. Sagittal sequences were obtained with 3–4 mm slice thickness and 11–15 slices were acquired. Sequence parameters were: 1. T1-weighted spin echo [repetition time (TR) 517–618 ms, echo time (TE) 13 ms)]; 2. STIR [(TR 3000–3170 ms, inversion time (TI) 140 ms, TE 38–61 ms)]. Field of view was 380 to 400 mm and matrix was 512 x 256 pixels. The spine was imaged in 2 parts: 1. Upper half comprising the entire cervical and most of the thoracic spine; 2. Lower half comprising the lower portion of the thoracic spine and entire lumbar spine. The specific MRI parameters for acquiring spinal images are provided on our website (www.arthritisdoctor.ca).

**Statistics.** The same MRI scans had been scored a year earlier by 2 experienced SPARCC readers, and these scores constituted the benchmark for the assessment of reliability. Interobserver reliability of the SPARCC MRI spine and SIJ scores between the rheumatology fellow and SPARCC readers was assessed with the intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC), which was calculated using an analysis of variance model, with total SPARCC score as the dependent variable, and patient and reader (fixed factor) as independent variables. An ICC value > 0.6, > 0.8, and > 0.9 indicates good, very good, and excellent reproducibility, respectively. We calculated ICC values for baseline and 12-week scores, as well as the change in score from baseline to 12 weeks.

**RESULTS**

Training required about 1 hour per reader for each module even though English was not the first language of any of the 3 readers. An improvement in ICC values for interobserver reliability of SPARCC SIJ inflammation scores was observed with all combinations of inexperienced reader with SPARCC reader save one (Table 1). Less improvement was evident in status scores, but ICC values were already high prior to training.

Improvement was also evident after training with the spine module, although both status and change score ICC values for spinal inflammation scores were already high prior to training (Table 2). The greatest improvement was observed for both status and change in the 23 DVU score.

DISCUSSION

These MRI reading exercises conducted by rheumatology fellows with minimal prior training in MRI demonstrate the following observations. First, we show that a simple Web-based training module requiring minimal time is an effective knowledge transfer technique. Second, we show that training in the use of the SPARCC scoring methods can be so effective that inexperienced readers score as reliably as the original developers of the SPARCC methods. Third, we demonstrate the importance of standardization of imaging protocol, methodological details, and definitions relevant to features of inflammation. Fourth, the high pretraining reliability scores for spinal inflammation attest to the methodological simplicity, feasibility, comprehension, and therefore external validity of the SPARCC MRI spine inflammation score.

Reader A had the least experience with MRI prior to this reading exercise, and the improvement in both status and change score ICC values after training was therefore not surprising. However, improvement in change score ICC values after training was noted for all readers. It is more difficult to attain high reliability for change scores as measured by the ICC, but it is also more desirable since the primary purpose of such an instrument is to reliably detect change and to discriminate effectively between treatment interventions. The ICC estimates the proportion of variance in the data that is due to differences between the subjects rather than differences between the readers, and as such reflects the concept of the signal-to-noise ratio. Consequently, since the differences between patients in status scores are much greater than differences in change scores, ICC values will tend to be lower on methodological grounds. Assessment of the reliability of change scores is therefore a more rigorous test of training. The greater improvement in the reliability of the 23 DVU score compared to the 6 DVU score is not surprising because the 6 DVU score focuses on the most severely affected lesions. Training is required to reliably detect the more subtle lesions. This also accounts for the high pretraining ICC values for the 6 DVU score. In other words, excellent reliability was evident with minimal training.

The principal limitation of the study is that the Web-based training modules were not compared with “usual” training in MRI. A problem with this approach, however, is that most rheumatology fellows do not receive any formal training in musculoskeletal MRI during the course of most fellowship training programs. The external validity of such a study would therefore be questionable. It is possible that a component of the improvement may reflect recall from the pretraining readings even though MRI scans were identified only by study number and scans were read 1 month apart. The retention of knowledge was also not studied, so it is unknown how long readers will maintain their expertise if training is not reinforced.

The post-training reliability data compare favorably with prior studies where these methods were used. A multitreader exercise using a similar study design conducted among rheumatologist and radiologist experts in MRI reported reliability data on several methods proposed for the quantitative assessment of spinal inflammation by MRI\textsuperscript{4}. The highest and most consistent ICC values were reported for the SPARCC method, but the median ICC value for change scores (0.78, range 0.42–0.93) was less than that noted in our present study after training. This multi-reader exercise\textsuperscript{4} was not preceded by formal standardiza-

tion of scoring methodology or training exercises and suggests that substantial improvement in reliability would be possible even among experts in the field. The importance of standardization of methodology, availability of reference images, and calibration of readers has been emphasized in the development of a scoring system to evaluate inflammation and structural damage by MRI in patients with rheumatoid arthritis by the OMERACT MRI group\textsuperscript{5}. Although this group has developed a set of reference images and has standardized the methodological approach, a formal mechanism to facilitate calibration and knowledge transfer has not yet been proposed.

In conclusion, we developed Web-based training modules aimed at standardization of the SPARCC MRI scoring methodology for assessing the extent of inflammation in the SIJ and spine in patients with SpA. These modules were shown to be effective in improving reliability to a level where inexperienced rheumatology fellows were able to evaluate active inflammatory lesions to a degree of reliability comparable to that of experienced SPARCC readers. This is therefore an effective approach to formalization of the process of calibration between readers. We recommend the use of these modules prior to application of the SPARCC MRI scoring methods and the adoption of a similar approach by developers of other instruments based on imaging modalities, to facilitate the process of knowledge transfer.

Wheat transport
Drought conditions in eastern Australia have cut GrainCorp’s receival figures in Queensland and NSW by roughly 73% year-on-year. It says drought conditions have led to a significant share of production remaining on farm or delivered directly to domestic consumers. With the 2018/19 harvest effectively complete, Queensland growers have fed 107,100 tonnes of grain into GrainCorp’s receival network, down more than 80% from the 557,250 tonnes recorded in 2017/18. In NSW, receivals have dropped 71%, from 2.08 million tonnes to 605,600 tonnes. The harvest continues in Victoria, with 1.28 million tonnes so far received. 2.94 million tonnes was received in 2017/18.

GrainCorp warned in October that drought conditions experienced in recent months would have a significant impact on volumes. Agribusiness bank Rabobank also warned of the scale of the drought’s impact on grain volumes in its Winter Crop Production Outlook. The outlook forecast a national harvest of just 29.3 million tonnes in the 2018/19 winter crop season, down 23%. “For vast regions of the eastern states, there will be no harvest, and where there is a harvest, yields will be anywhere between 30% and 50% down on average,” the report said.

In WA there has been a good year this season with a 13.3 million tonne wheat harvest.

Sydney Airport station fees
The amount of money the NSW government collects from fees paid by passengers using Sydney’s Airport stations has passed $100 million a year due to soaring patronage. A 16% increase in revenue from fees last financial year takes the total amount collected over the past four years to $310 million. A one-way train trip between Sydney’s CBD and the airport costs an adult passenger $18.70 during peak hours. Of that, $14.30 is the station access fee.

The latest payments to the state are detailed in annual accounts filed with the corporate regulator by the private operator of the two stations. Under a revised contract, the government is entitled to 85% of the sales revenue from Airport Link Company since July 2014, almost all of which comes from the station access fee.

The private operator said in the filings that it "expects continued revenue growth and positive cash flows" in the new financial year and beyond. It means the government will reap larger windfalls from the access fee in the coming years. More than 33,000 passengers pass through the station gates on a busy day, a 42% increase on 2012. The access fee for the airport stations is capped at $29 a week for Adult Opal card holders who travel by train to the airport more than once a week. Some passengers avoid paying the fee by catching trains to Wolli Creek or Mascot, and walking to the international and domestic terminals, or by catching buses.

Transport for NSW said there were no plans to remove the station access fee, arguing it would cost hundreds of millions of dollars. "All funds collected under the agreement with Airport Link go towards public transport improvements like more services and better infrastructure," it said. Labor has promised to cut the fee to $5, and abolish it altogether for the tens of thousands who work at the airport, if it wins the state election in March.

Sydney Airport has long argued for a cut to the fee to encourage more people to opt for public transport, in a bid to reduce pressure on nearby roads. The airport forecasts the number of passengers flying in and out of Kingsford Smith to increase by 51% to almost 66 million over the next two decades.

The boost to the government's coffers from the access fee for the airport stations is offset by the state having to "compensate" the private operator for passengers using Green Square and Mascot stations. That compensation was estimated at $22 million in 2016. The access fees at those stations have been subsidised by the government since 2011. Under the public-private partnership, Airport Link Company's rights to operate the four stations on the line ends in 2030, when their ownership will revert to the state.

**Victoria: Myki trap**

Not exactly a timetable story, but important for our members to know:

Just seconds after he touched on his myki and discovered the card had expired, a traveller was slapped with a $237 fine. "Who even knows that myki cards expire?" he said. When he touched on, a message flashed up on the myki reader saying the card had expired. There was no expiry date on the card itself and he had not received any alerts. Before he got a chance to get off the tram, plain-clothes ticket inspectors nabbed him, fining him for using an expired myki, despite more than $9 being loaded on his card. He is now taking Public Transport Victoria to court to fight the fine, arguing that he was unfairly punished.

Public Transport Users Association's spokesman Tony Morton said this was a case of a person "doing all the right things but getting caught out on an administrative detail". He questioned how anyone could think it appropriate to fine someone who has put money on their myki card and touched on, therefore "signalling their full intent to pay. What we now have is this situation where your myki is a ticking time bomb because it can catch you and when it does, you are liable to a fine," Mr Morton said. Four years was a short lifespan for a card, he said, especially in comparison with the ten year lifespan of Queensland's Go Card and Melbourne's old Metcard, which had no expiry date.

A Public Transport Victoria spokesman declined to comment on the case, but said authorised officers, conductors and other ticketing staff can provide information to passengers about expiry dates, which can also be viewed on myki vending machines and at ticket office terminals. "Just like everyday items including passports, drivers licences and bank cards, myki cards need to be replaced after a certain period of time to ensure maximum security," a spokesman said. "Expired mykis are replaced free of charge when they expire - we encourage passengers to speak to staff at a premium or staffed station or get in touch with the public transport call centre to arrange a replacement."

NTT Data, the company that developed myki, won a seven-year contract worth $700 million in 2016 to continue running the ticket system.

The Victorian government is currently carrying out trials of contactless technology for myki.

Metro Trains Melbourne: Dandenong line delay 14 January

On 14 January, in extreme heat, the Cranbourne and Pakenham lines were blocked in the evening peak by a faulty train at Carnegie. It is believed the fault originated with new overhead equipment just installed near Malvern. There were delays of up to two hours as buses replaced trains. Metro Trains advised passengers to board Frankston line trains and change to a replacement bus at Caulfield or "defer travel plans or seek alternative transport options". As a result the Frankston line experienced major delays of up to 90 minutes as Cranbourne-Pakenham passengers crammed on to its trains. Frankston line services returned to normal after 2000. The faulty train was removed shortly before 0500 next morning. However, other trains were now out of place and further delays ensued.

WA rail access

The WA government has proposed to make it easier for third parties to use the state’s railways. A draft decision paper proposes changes to the state’s Rail Access Regime to make it operate more efficiently and effectively, while maintaining flexibility for the diverse range of railways in the state.

Reviews of the Access Regime by the state’s Economic Regulation Authority in 2011 and 2015 raised considerable concerns that it does not provide an effective alternative when private negotiations fail between network operators and third parties.

The State government says it’s been consulting with stakeholders over the past 12 months on potential reforms to the Regime to help make it more effective. Submissions on the impact of the decision paper’s proposed changes are sought by 25 February. The

- TT83 - 491 Butler-Yanchep now extended to Yanchep Beach Road near Parkland Drive.

On 23 January Qantas and Jetstar announced 35 return flights from Darwin and other ports will be cut between April and late June. This will include Qantas routes to Sydney and Brisbane and Jetstar routes to Melbourne, Adelaide and Cairns. Qantas said the decision was based on a decline in demand, which it put down to a reduction in corporate travel and the wind-down of the Inpex gas plant's construction phase. It said there had been a 10% drop in demand in domestic flights to and from Darwin in recent months, and expected the trend to continue until the end of June. Recent tourism figures placed the Territory's tourism sector as the worst in the nation.

In November 2018, Qantas scrapped direct flights from Cairns to Alice Springs and Darwin and five weekly flights from Perth to Alice Springs and four from Perth to Darwin. However Qantas also added two weekly flights from Darwin and Adelaide to Uluru, and a new discount program for Alice Springs residents flying to Darwin.

Royal Brunei Airlines will recommence services to/from Brisbane with four services weekly from 11 June to Bandar Seri Begawan.

Thanks to the Australian for Air news.

St David's Lleithyr Meadow Club Campsite

Explore the Pembrokeshire Coast

Places to see and things to do in the local area

Make the most of your time

Visit

1. **Carew Castle**
   Once home to lords and ladies, now home to ghost stories and rare bats.

2. **Whitesands Bay Beach**
   An expanse of golden sand, one of the country’s finest surf beaches.

3. **Thousand Islands Expeditions**
   Enjoy a boat trip around Ramsey Island, passing by hidden bays, dramatic sea cliffs and wildlife. Also trips to Skomer Island to see the puffins.

4. **St David’s Cathedral**
   This magnificent, incredibly historic place of worship will take your breath away with its outstanding beauty.

5. **TYF Adventures for Life**
   Discover secret caves and hidden beach coves as you kayak or coasteer along St David’s coastline.

6. **Voyages of Discovery**
   An exhilarating day out as you navigate the dramatic shoreline of Ramsey Island on a rib boat and discover a wealth of wildlife along the way.

7. **Wild About Pembrokeshire**
   Foraging sessions with experts who will fuel your interest and captivate all ages.

Don’t forget to check your Great Savings Guide for all the latest offers on attractions throughout the UK.
camc.com/greatsavingsguide

Walk

8 Local Walks
The site is in the Pembrokeshire National Park, and there’s so much to see and do on foot. The coastal path is only a short walk, from Whitesands follow the path to the right to St David’s Head where you may spot seals, dolphins, porpoises, choughs, wild horses and even a pair of peregrine falcons.

Cycle

9 National Cycle Network
The nearest National Cycle Network route to this site is route number 4, The Celtic Trail West.

Local Routes
Information on local cycle routes from site is available from the information room.

Explore

10 St David’s
Steeped in history, Britain’s smallest city is famous for its spectacular cathedral, which was the final resting place of the Patron Saint of Wales.

11 Solva
This harbour village boasts a beautiful quay and charming, quirky high street.

12 Pembroke
A charming walled town home to a Norman Castle and rich, medieval heritage; the birthplace of Henry VII.

13 Lllys Y Fran Lake
Enjoy walking, cycling, fishing, kayaking, canoeing and paddleboarding.

Visitor’s top tips

The Bishops Pub in St David’s offers excellent food and a great selection of beers. It’s also dog-friendly.

For the freshest fish and chips try the famous ‘Shed Bistro’ in Porthgain.

You can book discounted boat trips to Ramsey or Skomer Island. Go via bus from outside the site (May to September) as parking is limited.

Walk or cycle to St David’s, the UK’s smallest city – the cathedral is not to be missed.

We loved the coastal walk to Whitesands, where you can see birds, sea life and wild ponies on Carn Llidi and St David’s Head.

There is a fantastic golf course under 5 minutes from site, which overlooks spectacular scenery.

The Blue Lagoon is a definite must-see in the area; bordered by gorgeous beaches and craggy cliffs, it is ideal for watersports.

The Marina in Pembroke Dock is a lovely spot to relax and soak up beautiful views.

Pembroke Castle is a brilliant visit, and the town is lovely too – full of interesting history.

You are spoiled for choice with over 50 stunning beaches, all linked via the Pembrokeshire Coastal Path.

Close by

Supermarkets
The closest is CK’s in St. David’s, about 1.5 miles.

Tourist Information
In St. David’s, 1.5 miles.

Swimming Pool
In Fishguard, 16 miles.

Cinema
In Fishguard, 16 miles.

Petrol Station
Texaco, 2.7 miles.

Cashpoint
There are three ATMs in St David’s, 2 miles.

Public transport
A bus stops at the site entrance and goes to St David’s and around the peninsula. From St David’s you can catch buses to Fishguard, Haverfordwest, Cardigan and Milford Haven (April to end of September).

Silvia Callorda, editor

Dear Commodores and Mariners

It has been a year since the change of International Commodore at the Melbourne convention and I must say that they have been excellent 12 months, in which I have worked very comfortably and in which I have had the pleasure of sharing with each of the members of the International Bridge. I wish to express my gratitude to each and every one of those who collaborate every day to make our fellowship this wonderful Rotary nautical world so special.

June is a particular month within the Rotary calendar, it is the month of Fellowships. This is how Rotary International recognizes the value and importance of the fellowships. Rotary has invited me to write an article about my experience in the Fellowship. It has been very pleasant to share my personal experiences and about IYFR in particular. It was published on the Service in Action Blog.

During this quarter, the AGM of the Italian fleets was held, which was a great success; and the AGM of Area 1 was held with a large influx of fleet members and which included a memorable navigation on the Danube River. Congratulations to the organizers of both events. I will not expand since they can be seen in articles in this Rotafloat.

Our fellowship has continued to grow. We have celebrated the founding of the Barcelona fleet. Congratulations to Fleet Coomodore Rosmarie Cammany and all its founding members. A new squadron has also been founded. It is the Singapore Squadron, led by Captain Leo Wang, who received his diploma during the Gala Dinner at the Singapore Convention. Our congratulations to all of them. However, our growth and good news do not end there, a new fleet in Area 2 is preparing.

The Singapore convention was held between May 25 and 29. Our fellowship has been represented at the House of Friendship with our booth. On the 29th the AGM and the Gala Dinner were held. Both events were held at the American Club in Singapore. On the 30th the PCC was held. It has been a very successful sail to Sentosa Island. We especially thank the team that organized the Convention activities with great affection and care: PIC Jun Avecilla, IRC Paul Yiu, A3C Edmond Aguilar and RC Arturo Supangan. Everything was magnificent, thank you very much dear friends! All of you will be able to appreciate in the articles and colorful photos that we are publishing, the friendship and warmth that reigned in all the activities.

Regarding the information released during the AGM, in the next pages you can see the reports of A1C Carlo d’Amore, A2C Miriam Caudullo, A3C Edmond Aguilar, the Plastic Free Waters Coordinator PIC Sergio Santi, and my annual report. I can anticipate that it is all good news.

Let’s continue enjoying our beloved IYFR. I wish you all good winds.
See you in September!

AREA 1 REPORT
A1C AND IVC CARLO d’AMORE

This IYFR Year has felt the repercussions of the war in Ukraine and the devastating effects of the Earthquakes in Turkey and Slovenia.

Nevertheless, our activity this year is back to pre-covid. We started the year with approximately 1600 Mariners in Area 1, divided into 13 Regions with 62 Fleets. Insofar as the “Year on Year”, we have experienced a growth of 5.75% for the first time in years, that is, a total of 1692 Mariners at end of April. Despite the actual recession, occurring in parts of Europe, we have been able, thanks to the hard work of our A1 Treasurer Annette Lewis, to collect dues. While there have been some exceptions, they are currently being addressed and may lead to disbanding some Fleets that have lost interest.

The Swiss Fleet has been busy with 4 events. In Iberia, the Southeaster Fleet has organized a visit to the Naval Academy in Galicia and a cruise to Ibiza, planned in June, among other events. On May 30th, the newly relaunched Barcelona Fleet will be chartered.

Italy, particularly the Rome Fleet and their Sardinia Squadron, has organized a belated, but highly successful 22-23 A1 AGM. The Balkans Region with its Serbian Fleet is preparing the A1 23-24 AGM in Belgrade, June 7th-10th with a small cruise on the Danube.

Greece&Israel have reported a Commodore change in Israel and several activities in the three Greek Fleets in regard to the Blueair initiative, the Fleet AGM and the traditional Cutting of New Year pie. Africa has seen a New RC and the chartering of a new Cote d’Ivoire fleet as well as renewed leadership in the Mali Fleet. Despite Ukraine being at war, it is still one of the most active Regions with a multitude of activities such as the Region AGM, Sailing Championship for juniors, Sailing trips to Sardinia, the signing of ICC Ukraine-Nederland, involving 7 districts, etc. Eastern Europe will have a Sailing week in May and a Polish-Ukrainian Sailing Youth Camp.

Germany collaborated with the Swiss Fleet insofar as Sailing in Sicily and Lake Garda, among other Meetings with all the Fleets of the Region. GB&I had a few new members this year. A major concern is their aging Fellowship membership. Consequently, they are working on attracting new members.

No other Regions have reported their activity as of April 24th.

The PFW initiative is still undergoing and in expansion. In closing, I would like to make special mention and express my sympathy once again to our Fellow Members and their Families and friends who are at war. We hope that peace can soon be achieved and that reconstruction may soon begin.

AREA 2 REPORT
A2C MIRIAM CAUDULLO

GENERALITIES:
Since the beginning of 2023-2024 period and, by virtue of the reform to our General Rules, we have Vice Commodore, the position is held by sailor Alfonso Bolio, member of the Cancun Ocean and Sand Fleet. The AVC actively fulfills its functions by permanently collaborating with this AC in communications with the fleets, especially those in North America.

For personal reasons, the appointed area treasurer had to leave his duties. Fleet Secretary Hugo Klopfstein of the Guayaquil Fleet, Ecuador was appointed to replace him. Also, since the beginning of the period, meetings have been implemented via Zoom with groups of Fleet Commodores by Region or by groups of Fleets. The objective of these meetings was to update issues related to the GR, particularly regarding its reforms and also on Rotary’s Policies on Fellowships.

AGM:
After four years without in-person meetings in the area due to the COVID 19 pandemic, Area 2 met again in person, this time it was in Cancún, Mexico, between March 6 and 9, 2024. It was a successful meeting, full of affection, fun and a solidarity action that took place in the pediatric oncology service of the Cancún Hospital; in the A2 meetings, the task of service has already become a habit. IC Angie Mesistrano was present and also PIC Jun Avecilla and his family. 7 fleets were represented.

MEMBERSHIP:
We are happy to have added a new fleet and one more flag, the Puerto Cabello Fleet of Venezuela has been formed.

In terms of number of members, unfortunately, we have decreased from March 2023 to May 2024, 15 members; the total membership of A2 is 672 members.

Unfortunately, the Chicago fleet was dissolved at the request of its own members.

It is worth noting that there are several squadrons in operation and some of them are on the way to reaching enough members to form a new fleet.

PUBLIC IMAGE:
The A2 Fleets disseminate their actions through their own web pages, social networks and in print and television media.

In some Districts, even in Zones, they have the opportunity to promote the fellowship and its actions at district meetings, also in Rotary Institutes.

FINANCES:
A2’s finances are in good health. At the time of preparing this report, which is carried out prior to the end of the 2023-2024 period, we are managing the collection of a fleet that fell behind.

FLEET ACTIVITIES:
The A2 fleets carry out regular meetings or navigations, some of the fleets, especially in Latin America, carry out service actions while having fun, they take first aid, CPR, nutrition, psychology workshops to island schools; others carry out nautical therapy to give access to nautical activities to people with disabilities; other fleets have navigation training programs and others are dedicated to beach cleaning.

In summary, in A2 our fellowship is working and enjoying Rotary friendship and camaraderie.

PLASTIC FREE WATERS PROGRAM
COORDINATOR PIC SERGIO SANTI

GHOST NETS SIGHTING AND REPORTING SYSTEM

The non-profit Rotarian Association IYFR (International Yachting Fellowship of Rotarians) is engaged in the Plastic Free Waters Operation which consists in trying to reduce the amount of plastic waste that is dumped daily into the lakes, seas and oceans of the Planet. The complete description of the activities and the international scope of the objectives and the strategies to achieve them are reported on the websites http://www.iyfr.net, https://www.iyfr.net/the-program/ and https://iyfroperationplasticfree-waters.org/.

One of the most conspicuous and harmful types of plastic waste (which can also be recovered and recycled) is made up of voluntarily or otherwise, abandoned fishing nets, both floating and on the seabed, since in addition to being a danger for navigation, especially yachting, constitute a useless deadly trap for aquatic animals.

While making the partial solution to this problem possible, IYFR at the same time wants to create awareness and a feeling of unity of purpose among all those who want to participate in this action to safeguard the navigation and the Planet.

For this reason, IYFR has financed the creation of a new APP for the sighting and tracking of the so-called "ghost nets", i.e. those fishing equipment that for any reason are abandoned at sea or into the lakes.

IYFR wants to offer the possibility to seafarers (in particular to yachtsmen but also to fishermen, tourists on cruise ships, private planes, underwater sportsmen, etc.) and to all those who happen to spot a net adrift or on the bottom, to provide its the photo(s) and geographical coordinates of the location of the sighting. The information, in the form of an email attachment, thanks to the PFW APP https://www.iyfr.net/app-plastic-free-waters/ is sent with a simple click to the relevant Operations Center of the Maritime Control Authority of the Area. The Marine Organization receives the photos and position data, processes them and, if necessary, arranges for the nets/bulge plastic relict recovery. It will then be the task of the specially equipped ships to complete the recovery and send the material for recycling according to national regulations and/or indications.

IYFR and, where this Fellowship is not represented, the Rotary International Districts have the delicate task of creating the same agreements with the relevant Authorities as it was done in 2023 in Italy by IYFR PFW. The Italian institutional interlocutor of choice for this initiative is the Coast Guard, as supervisory and coordinating Maritime Organization. The Companies responsible for recovery, at least in Italy, are already in action but, in the absence of this widespread source of information and related coordination, they carry out occasional patrolling activities starting from various ports or springs, with an obvious waste of resources and time.

IYFR PFW is already working on this topic with other National and Non-National Rotarian Organizations such as MARE NOSTRUM and END PLASTIC SOUP, with the leaders of the various Nautical Associations such as the Italian Naval League, MAREVIVO, the Fratelli della Costa, WWF, and other Associations and Yacht Club on the Italian coasts, who will inform their respective members of the initiative, giving it appropriate prominence.

The IYFR PFW APP has the following features:
- Works with Android and IOS operating systems;
- NO COST, simple and intuitive to install and activate, following user registration.
- Simplicity and intuitiveness of use.
- There are no complications or special skills required to use it.

Upon sighting a ghost net, the user can use the APP to detect its position (lat/long), take one or more photos, add, if necessary, further information (size of the wreck, on the surface/bottom) and, with a simple click send the report to the recipient Authority. In Italy the recipient is the Coast Guard as per the Agreement signed on 25 May 2023. The report will include the group, date and time of the sighting and the geographical coordinates of the point, as well as any comments (click on the intuitive iconography).

If the sighting occurs in an area of non-internet coverage, the APP will withhold the report, making subsequent sending attempts until a positive conclusion.

At the start of the activity, we foresee the following phases:
- Communication through paper and available media of the operability of the APP and the launch of the program (in one or more Countries).
- The dissemination, by the participating Organizations to their members, of information on the existence, operation of the Network, with indications of the purposes and importance of participation, and instructions on the content of the reports.
- The periodic dissemination of news on the reports received and the follow-up to them, and on the results of the activity, with the aim of encouraging constant awareness and participation in the campaign by seafarers and any other interested user.

Area 1: membership grew by 5.75%. They held many activities, held the last AGM in Serbia, with an excellent program, between June 7 and 9. Two new fleets have been founded. Cote d’Ivoire fleet, in Africa and Barcelona fleet, in Spain.

The traditional Gala Dinner took place in the rooms of the American Club. Every year, after the Gala Dinner, we conclude the formal activities related to the convention.

The 2024 International Maritime Safety Committee (IMSC) meeting was held in Manila, Philippines from May 13 to 17, 2024. The meeting was attended by representatives from various countries and organizations, including the International Maritime Organization (IMO), the International Association of Classification Societies (IACS), and the International Association of Marine Surveyors (IAMAS).

During the meeting, discussions focused on various maritime safety issues, including ship stability, ballast water management, and crew welfare. The participants also discussed the implementation of the International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS) and the International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL).

The meeting concluded with a series of resolutions and recommendations aimed at improving maritime safety and security. The participants agreed to continue working together to address the challenges facing the global shipping industry.

The meeting was hosted by the Department of Transportation of the Philippines and was attended by over 150 delegates from 36 countries. The event was well-attended and provided an excellent opportunity for delegates to network and share their experiences and expertise.

The meeting was a success and has set the stage for future international cooperation in the field of maritime safety. The participants have committed to continuing their efforts to improve maritime safety and security and to work towards a safer and more sustainable shipping industry.

AREA 1 ANNUAL GENERAL MEETING 2024
BELGRADE SERBIA, JUNE 7th -11th

By Marko Murn, Regional Commodore of Balkans

After pandemic of Covid we have succeeded to have again really international gathering of 52 participants from 13 states of Europe and USA. Even extreme hot and sunny weather could not kill the sincere friendship charge, our desire to socialize and have fun.

After sight seeing of Belgrade and the official AGM Office we had the gala dinner on a ship, cruising along confluence of rivers Sava and Danube, being surprised by great city fireworks and enjoying wonderful music, which local ensemble conjured up for us.

Serbia has no sea, but it lays on unique geographical position of the navigatable rivers Danube, Tisa, Sava and Morava. Together with many additional channels they form a remarkable network of inland waterways. There are hundreds kilometres of rivers, channels, reservoirs and lakes available to small recreational crafts, where each July the members of IYFR Serbia sail in flotilla visiting Rotary Clubs along their way.

We have learnt some specifics of river navigation and experienced the most beautiful cruise along Danube river from Belgrade to Kladovo, passing many historical fortresses, Roman remains of roads and bridges.

The Danube within the area of Iron Gate reaches a width of 6 km, which narrows to only 150 m in the gorge of Veliki Kazan. On the way back we visited also the archaeological site of Lepenski vir, one of the oldest urban settlements on the world from Middle Stone Age (6,500 BC).

Four days of intensive gatherings, having fun and forming new friendships passed so quick when we found ourselves in a moment of farewell with a sincere wish to definitely meet again at the next AGM in Kiel, Germany on August 8, 2025.

Thank you, our dear friends from Serbian fleet, for the excellent program and your warm hospitality!

AGM REGIONE ITALIA, BRINDISI 25 - 28 APRILE 2024

By Alessandro D’Onofrio, Fleet Commodore Italy South-East Fleet

Grazie alla perfetta organizzazione dei nostri amici di Brindisi, nei giorni precedenti i Mariners convenuti hanno potuto visitare il Castello Svevo, il centro storico della città, e anche di fare una navigazione nel bacino interno del porto di Brindisi, con visita all’altro castello, quello aragonese, e al Monumento al Marinaio d’Italia, che è il simbolo della città di Brindisi.

La sessione dell’AGM della Regione Italia sabato pomeriggio è stata dedicata al tema della Sicurezza nella navigazione da diporto, come previsto dal programma. I tre autorevoli Speakers sono stati i Mariners della Flotta Sud-Est Marco Tommas, esperto nazionale di Formazione per la sicurezza, e Roberto Sammarchi, avvocato, e il Mariner della Flotta Rome Massimiliano Oggiano, anche lui avvocato. Essi hanno parlato di necessità di formazione specifica per la sicurezza, della intesa di vari enti, compresa la IYFR Italia, per promuovere attivamente la evoluzione delle norme per rispondere a quelle necessità, e delle responsabilità penali del Comandante e del suo equipaggio secondo le ultime leggi in vigore da pochi mesi. Il FC Sandro D’Onofrio ha poi illustrato una nuova app prodotta a cura della IYFR, per segnalare la presenza di reti fantasma, e gli accordi sottoscritti con la Guardia Costiera italiana per la attivazione dell’invio di questi report.

Il FC Sandro D’Onofrio introduce il tema e preseta i relatori

La assemblea si è svolta sotto la guida dell’RC Giuseppe Garrisi, ed hanno partecipato anche il PIC Sergio Santi e il nuovo RC Nello Catalano, che assumerà le sue funzioni il 1° Luglio

Durante la cena di gala che è seguita sono stati accolti nella IYFR quattro nuovi Mariners, nella Flotta Sud-Est e in quella di Roma.

AGM ITALY REGION, BRINDISI 25 – 28 APRIL 2024

Thanks to the perfect organization of our friends from Brindisi, in the previous days the Mariners gathered were able to visit the Castello Svevo, the historic center of the city, and also to sail in the internal basin of the port of Brindisi, with a visit to the other castle, the Aragonese one, and the Monument to the Sailor of Italy, which is the symbol of the city of Brindisi.

The session of the AGM of the Italian Region on Saturday afternoon was dedicated to the theme of Safety in recreational sailing, as foreseen by the programme. The three authoritative speakers were the Mariners of the South-East Fleet Marco Tommas, national expert in safety training, and Roberto Sammarchi, lawyer, and the Mariner of the Rome Fleet Massimiliano Oggiano, also a lawyer. They spoke about the need for specific training for safety, of the agreement of various bodies, including IYFR Italia, to actively promote the evolution of the rules to respond to those needs, and of the criminal responsibilities of the Captain and his crew according to the latest laws in force for a few months. The assembly took place under the leadership of the RC Giuseppe Garrisì, and the PIC Sergio Santi and the new RC Nello Catalano, who will take up his duties on July 1st, also participated.

During the gala dinner that followed, four new Mariners were welcomed into the IYFR, in the South-East Fleet and in the Rome Fleet.

HELENIC IONIAN FLEET ACTIVITIES

By Aris Zachos, Fleet Commodore

Hellenic Ionian Fleet, with PDG Aris Zachos as Founding Commodore, was founded in the Summer of 2015, presenting for the first time in Greece our great Fellowship and its advantages. In a rapid progress, our Fleet contributed to the founding of two additional Fleets in Greece, and a Squadron in Montenegro.

Base of the Fleet is the port of Preveza in Amvrakikos Gulf, important sailing center of NW Greece. Its activities extend to the Ionian Sea, and to the land area of Epirus (lakes, rivers). In addition to Preveza, the Fleet is represented in the ports of Corfu & Lefkas islands, and in the ports of Thessaloniki & Kavala (North Aegean Sea).

The 20 members of the Fleet include five boat owners, while three more members have a direct professional relationship with marine recreation.

The main supporter of the Fleet is R.C. Preveza, followed by businesses of the city, while we work closely with Marinas, Municipal Services, the Chamber of Commerce, the Maritime Academy of Epirus, the Professional Fishermen's Association and other bodies.

In addition to the Fellowship Events on land and at sea, the Fleet implements specific actions:

Permanent actions & programs include:
• information campaigns (general public),
• educational actions (Youth),
• reception - hospitality - support of passing sailors (technical & tourist matters),
• organization of sailing trips in the wider area,
• environmental actions, such as:
  - coast / beaches cleaning
  - cleaning / beautification of
  - coastal areas & river’s banks,
  - interventions in Amvrakikos Gulf to protect the marine ecosystem and clean up coasts, where accessible,
• development initiatives (technical consultant for local programs, plan - proposal of R.C. Preveza for the holistic utilization of the port of Preveza).

Now, our Fleet is ready to adopt and join the Plastic Free Waters project of IYFR.

We are waiting to welcome you all for joint actions on the field or for your simple recreation in one of the most attractive areas of the Mediterranean.

IYFR REGION UKRAINE. CHALLENGES OF WAR

By Olha Paliychuk, Region Commodore Ukraine Region

Are you aware of how the Ukrainian IYFR Fleets live after the Russian invasion on 24, February 2022?

You’ve probably all heard that with the outbreak of hostilities in Ukraine, all the Black Sea ports and river approaches were blocked. It’s entirely clear that under these conditions any navigation including sailing turned out to be impossible. And if in peacetime all four Ukrainian IYFR with their squadrons could be proud of charity sailing regattas in favour of children’s sailing schools, nowadays they faced with challenge of how to ensure the survival of those schools in the conditions of constant missile attacks on water areas. In those days it wasn’t so much about help on indoor training, but more about evacuating children to safe places.

Pretty fast the “Love Save Ukraine-Help Young Sailors” program was created and came into force.

From the first days of war until now we were able to organize several training two and three week camps for students in such safe places.

Thanks to the Ukrainian IYFR fleet activists, as well as the financial support of our foreign friends from IYFR fleets and Rotary Clubs literally from the first days of war until now we were able to organize several training two and three week camps for students in such safe places like Yuzhny Harbour near Odesa in Ukraine, Bjala in Bulgaria, Biograd na Moru in Croatia, Cherkasy dam water area in Ukraine.

We are infinitely grateful for the support of our program to IYFR officers Sergio Santi, David Hansen, Angie Mesistranto, Carlo d’Amore, Giuseppe Garris, Gordan Borcilo, Bob and Ann Burns, to Ivan Miskulin and Suncica Bulat from Croatian Rotary District 1913, Scottish RC Gourock and Greenock, Rotarians from Austin, Texas, Disaster Aid UK & Ireland, German IYFR, RC Stade and few private Ukrainian Companies from Mykolaiv and Cherkasy.

Our program is far from finished and the forthcoming summer 2024 poses new challenges before IYFR Ukraine. We don’t give up and intend to keep our work going on.

NEWS FROM SEATTLE ROTARY MARINERS

By Alan Bergen, Past Fleet Commodore

The Seattle Rotary Mariners do the Duck Dodge summer race series on Seattle’s Lake Union. Each Tuesday there are seventy to ninety boats competing. The emphasis is more on fun than racing. Traditionally we’ll have up to three boats racing. After the race, many of the boats raft up for a cocktail party that lasts until midnight. There’s even a dock party at a member’s houseboat for the land lovers. Whatever your preference, it’s a great time for everyone.

For a little more background Lake Union is a fresh water lake near downtown Seattle. You may already know of it. It played a prominent role in the movie "Sleepless in Seattle".

The houseboat used during the filming of the movie still exists and it is somewhat of a local landmark. In addition, the movie "Boys in the Boat" tells the story of the nearby University of Washington crew and their hard row to the gold medal in the 1936 Olympic Sports. We also use the lake as an airport. Sea Planes headed for Canada land and take off here ever until 7PM.

We love our lake so much we are always looking for new ways to have fun on it. Floating hot tubs, electric boat pizza parties, laser art shows and Independence Day fireworks all come to mind. Among the other major events are the Opening Day of Boating Season. It features Parties at the Seattle Yacht Club, crew races and a boat parade by the fifty yacht clubs in attendance. Of course. The Seattle Rotary Mariners always find a way to participate.

Stay tuned!

FLOTA MONTEVIDEO
"UN SACUDON AL ALMA"

Por María del Carmen Liesegang, Oficial Historiadora

¿Por qué la navegación es terapéutica para el cuerpo y para el alma?
Te responderé por boca de una sabia mujer:
"Cuando navego el pasado deja de entristecerme y el futuro de preocuparme; logro paz, mente en blanco y mi corazón se calma"...
La recreación es un derecho inalienable por ser parte de la salud integral de toda persona y la navegación, indudablemente, una excelente terapia!
Por tal razón Flota Montevideo, su Comodoro Andrés Almansa y Club Náutico La Estacada se reunieron en el punto más austral del Uruguay, Punta Carretas, para tejer inéditas redes de Servicio, Paz y Compañerismo con la estrella de la Inclusión como norte.

Escuela de vela

Un sacudón al alma" es la emoción del primer paseo marítimo de una niña cuando pregunta, puedo tocar el agua...o la amplia sonrisa del joven ciego cuando exclama, fuiste tú, quien me mojó pues al viento lo tengo de frente...o la felicidad del maquinista de piernas amputadas navegando por ese mar que un día tuvo que abandonar...

Personas con capacidades diferentes – sordomudos.

¿Nuestra ruta?
- Promover una Consciencia Ambiental libre de plásticos en las aguas y aledaños.
- Democratizar el Deporte Náutico Recreativo en jóvenes de contextos críticos, personas con capacidades diferentes y de la "cuarta edad".

Actividades náuticas recreativas, deportivas, sociales, culturales, talleres de capacitación, Escuela de Vela para niños y padres etc. gratuitamente se brindan a la comunidad. Y desde mayo, el Prof. Juan Andino comenzó a dar clase de Judo a ciegos, que entusiastas responden, inspirados en el No vidente Henry Borges, quien representará por 5ta vez a Uruguay en las Paralímpicas 2024 de París.

El sueño de convertir a "La Estacada" en el Primer Club Náutico de América del Sur 100 % Inclusivo inspira y sostiene a su Pte. Gustavo Moneta, hombre de negocios con profunda vocación de servicio que junto al precursor de la Náutico Terapia en el Uruguay Cap. Juan Alonso y al Dr. Adolfo Idoyaga Instructor de la Escuela Náutica constituyen la invaluable trilogía con la que codo a codo luchamos para llegar a buen puerto...

Montevideo Fleet, its Commodore Andrés Almansa and Club Náutico La Estacada met in Punta Carretas, to weave unprecedented networks of Service, Peace and Companionship.

Our route?
- Promote an Environmental Consciousness free of plastics in the waters and surrounding areas.
- Democratize Recreational Nautical Sport in young people from critical contexts, people with different abilities and the "fourth age".
Nautical recreational, sports, social, cultural activities, training workshops, Sailing School for children and parents, etc. They are provided free of charge to the community. And since May, Prof. Juan Andino began teaching Judo classes to the blind, who enthusiastically respond, inspired by the blind Henry Borges, who will represent Uruguay for the 5th time in the 2024 Paralympics in Paris.

"A jolt to the soul" is the emotion of a girl's first boardwalk when she asks, can I touch the water...or the wide smile of the young blind man when he exclaims, it was you, who got me wet because I have the wind in front of me...or the happiness of the engineer with amputated legs sailing through that sea that one day he had to abandon...

The dream of turning "La Estacada" into the First 100% inclusive Nautical Club in South America inspires and sustains its President Gustavo Moneta, a businessman with a deep vocation for service who, together with the precursor of Nautical Therapy in Uruguay Captain Juan Alonso and Dr. Adolfo Idoyaga, Instructor of the Nautical School, constitute the invaluable trilogy with which we fight side by side to reach a successful conclusion...

Enlisting more sailors is an imperative, for this reason on July 27 we will welcome on board the First Boyfriends of our Nascent Squadron, made up of young people from the environment and from Interact clubs!

Together, saying and doing, Flota Montevideo and Club Náutico "La Estacada" navigate for an Inclusive Nautical Sport and an Environmental Consciousness free of plastics in bodies of water and surrounding areas!

En abril de 2024, tuve la extraordinaria oportunidad de embarcarme en la goleta de tres mástiles Oosterschelde, una embarcación holandesa que habíamos contactado con la IYFR durante su escala en Punta del Este, Uruguay en 2023.

Construida en 1917 y conservada en su estado original, evoca la época de la navegación clásica, con mecanismos manuales para izar velas y manejo colaborativo. No sólo es un barco, sino un vivo testimonio de la historia marítima.

Este viaje formaba parte del proyecto Darwin200, una iniciativa global para despertar conciencia sobre la protección ambiental y la conservación de especies en peligro de extinción, replicando el viaje que Charles Darwin realizara a bordo del Beagle en 1830. Ver más información en https://darwin200.com/.

El trayecto implica dos años de navegación, dividida en varias piernas. Yo navegué desde el Callao, Perú, hasta Puerto Lucía, Ecuador, a través del vasto Océano Pacífico.

A bordo, la tripulación de 10 personas y los 20 pasajeros nos unimos en la pasión por el mar y el respeto por la naturaleza.

Durante el día, izábamos velas, timoneábamos y observábamos la vida marina: delfines juguetones y aves majestuosas nos permitían apreciar la biodiversidad del océano.

Las noches eran memorables. Bajo un cielo estrellado y lejos de las luces urbanas, compartíamos nuestras profesiones e intereses personales con compañeros de todo el mundo, creando un espíritu de comunidad.

NAVIGATING THROUGH TIME AND NATURE: A JOURNEY ABOARD THE TALL SHIP OOSTERSCHELDE

By: Ricardo Aguerre, Punta del Este Fleet Officer, IYFR

In April 2024, I had the extraordinary opportunity to embark on the three-masted schooner *Oosterschelde*, a Dutch tall ship we had contacted with the IYFR during its stop in Punta del Este, Uruguay, in 2023.

Built in 1917 and preserved in its original state, it evokes the era of classic sailing, with manual mechanisms for hoisting sails and collaborative handling. It is not just a ship but a vivid testament to maritime history.

This journey was part of the Darwin200 project, a global initiative to raise awareness about environmental protection and the conservation of endangered species, replicating the voyage that Charles Darwin made aboard the Beagle in 1830. For more information, see https://darwin200.com/.

The voyage involves two years of navigation, divided into several legs. I sailed from Callao, Peru, to Puerto Lucía, Ecuador, across the vast Pacific Ocean.

Onboard, the crew of 10 and the 20 passengers shared the passion for the sea and respect for nature.

During the day, we hoisted sails, steered the ship, and observed marine life: playful dolphins and majestic birds allowed us to appreciate the ocean's biodiversity.

The nights were memorable. Under a starry sky, far from urban lights, we shared our professions and personal interests with other passengers and crew members, from around the world and all walks of life, creating a spirit of community.

The navigation was calm. Serene seas invited contemplation, and moments of scarce wind required the use of the engines. Each experience, whether steering or simply observing the ocean, left a profound impression on everyone.

This journey allowed us to observe the beauty and fragility of our environment and value the importance of environmental protection initiatives actively promoted by the IYFR.

This experience was, without any doubt, unforgettable for those who love the sea and feel committed to preserve its integrity.

NAVEGACION EN EL VELERO “ESPERANZA”
DE PREFECTURA NAVAL ARGENTINA

By Miriam Caudullo, Area 2 Commodore

En una mañana fría y soleada, una delegación de Flota Delta de Argentina, salió a navegar por las aguas color de león del Río de la Plata en el velero de la Prefectura Naval Argentina “Esperanza”. La cita fue en el Yacht Club Puerto Madero. El Capitán del “Esperanza”, Prefecto Alejandro Lanciotti, recibió a esta delegación, brindó algunas breves explicaciones sobre la nave, sobre la navegación y seguridad. Oficiales de la Prefectura Naval Argentina acompañaron la travesía. Alrededor de las 10 y 30 se soltaron amarras y la embarcación dio una vuelta por el dique cuatro de Puerto Madero, donde está fondeada, aguardando la apertura de la exclusa que ocurrió a las 11 horas. Una vez que se atravesó la exclusa, pasó frente a la magnífica Fragata ARA Libertad, por inmediaciones del Yacht Club Argentino y luego, se pasó el ante puerto para ya salir al río con dirección SE, el escenario mostraba diversas regatas que tenían lugar en el horizonte. Con buen viento de dirección SO, la velocidad alcanzada llegó a los 8.5 nudos, el velero se deslizó por las aguas con mucha suavidad y haciendo gala de su magnífico porte. A medida que pasaban las horas, el viento se tornó cada vez más intenso y llegó el momento de volver. De regreso a Puerto Madero, todos tuvieron un merecido almuerzo criollo-marinero en el que estuvieron presentes agradables conversaciones y historias náuticas.

El “Esperanza” es un velero clásico de madera; eslora de 18,84 metros, manga de 4,21 metros y cala 2,65 metros; aparejo yawl, superficie vélica total de 196 m²; diseño de Roberto Hosmann, fue botado el 20 de diciembre de 1968. Su misión es servir de instrucción especializada en navegación a vela y representa a la Prefectura en regatas y eventos náuticos, habiendo obtenido diversos premios. Muchas gracias Prefectura Naval Argentina por haber recibido a Flota Delta en esta mañana!!!

A bordo del “Esperanza” en la amara del Yacht Club Puerto Madero. / Delegation aboard the “Esperanza” in the harbor of the Puerto Madero Yacht Club.

NAVIGATION ON THE SAILBOAT “ESPERANZA” OF THE ARGENTINE NAVAL PREFECTURE

On a, but sunny morning, a delegation of Delta Fleet (Argentina) sailed through the lion-colored waters of the Río de la Plata on the Argentine Coast Guard sailboat “Esperanza.” The appointment was at 10 in the morning at the Yacht Club Puerto Madero. The Captain of the “Esperanza”, Prefect Alejandro Lanciotti, received this delegation and gave some brief explanations about the ship, navigation and safety would be like. Officers from the Argentine Coast Guard accompanied the journey. Around 10:30 a.m. the moorings were released and the boat went around dock four of Puerto Madero, where it is anchored, awaiting the opening of the lock, which occurred at 11 a.m. Once the lock was crossed, it passed in front of the magnificent ARA Libertad Frigate, near the Argentine Yacht Club and then reached the river in a SE direction. With good wind from the SW direction, the speed reached reached 8.5 knots, the sailboat slid through the waters very smoothly and showing off its magnificent poise. As the hours passed, the wind became increasingly intense and it was time to return. Back in Puerto Madero, everyone had a well-deserved Creole-sailor lunch in which pleasant conversations and nautical stories were present.

The “Esperanza” is a classic sailboat made of wood; It has a length of 18.84 meters, a beam of 4.21 meters and a draft of 2.65 meters; yawl rig and a total sail area of 196 m²; She is a design by Roberto Hosmann and she was launched on December 20, 1968. Her mission is to serve as specialized instruction in sailing navigation for the students of the Training Institutes of the Argentine Naval Prefecture; She also represents the Coast Guard in regattas and nautical events, having obtained various awards.

Thank you very much Argentine Coast Guard for having received Flota Delta this morning!!!

UNA NUEVA FLOTA EN AREA 2
Y NUEVA BANDERA EN IYFR:
VENEZUELA PUERTO CABELLO FLEET

By Nonay Riera, Fleet Rear Commodore

Nuestra primera reunión presencial celebrada el día 07 de Junio, con motivo del acto de recibimiento de la carta constitutiva de la Flota de Puerto Cabello de la IYFR, se inició con la lectura de nuestro Vice Comodoro, Albin Solarte de la carta de salutación de nuestra Comodoro Internacional, Angélica (Angie) Mesistrano, nuestra Contra Comodoro, Nonay Riera leyó la carta de la Comodoro de Area 2 Miriam Caudullo, Paul Patacho presentó el video de nuestro Vice Comodoro Internacional Carlo D’Amore, y diferentes Comodoros y past comodoros de otras flotas, fuimos honrados con la presencia de Roberto Ramírez Gobernador del Distrito 4380, dos Distritos, 4370 y 4380, una sola flota.

Al día siguiente nos dirigimos al Parque Nacional Morrocoy para nuestra primera navegación, con espíritu de camaradería y cercanía que parecía que nos unían millas de navegación. Invitamos a todos a navegar en nuestro país, nuestra gente es más cálida que las aguas del mar caribe que nos baña.

En esta primera experiencia socios del 4380 apoyaron a una actividad del Distrito 4370, saliendo líneas de acción de servicio para nuestro próximo encuentro. Parafraseando un hermoso poema, “el amor y la amistad son simples, y las cosas simples las devora el tiempo”, así que a dedicar lo más valioso que podemos regalarnos a nosotros y a otros, reservarnos el tiempo. Estamos seguros que las agrupaciones rotarias, son la vía por excelencia para el fortalecimiento y crecimiento de la membresía.

The night of our first face-to-face meeting celebrated on June 7th, on the formal act of receiving our charter of the Puerto Cabello Fleet of the IYFR, began with the reading of our Vice Commodore, Albin Solarte of the greeting letter of our International Commodore, Angelica (Angie) Mesistrano, our Rear Commodore, Nonay Riera read the letter of the Area 2 Commodore Miriam Caudullo, Paul Patacho presented the video of our International Vice Commodore Carlo D’Amore, and different Commodors and past Commodors from other fleets, we were honored with the presence of Roberto Ramirez Governor of District 4380, two Districts, 4370 and 4380, a single fleet.

At this first meeting there was a brief presentation of each of the members of the fleet, reaching the conclusion that Rotary invites us to a friendship with purpose, but Rotary Fellowships invite us to enjoy that friendship even more, making the analogy that Rotary Fellowships come to be a music channel where the algorithm puts us our favorite pieces and it is in that atmosphere of harmony and joy that great opportunities for service are propitiated.

The next day we headed to the Morrocoy National Park, for our first sailing activity, with a tremendous spirit of camaraderie and a closeness that seemed to unite us miles of sailing, but this closeness is very own and particular of Venezuelans, so we invite you to a sailing adventure in our country, where our people are warmer than the waters of the Caribbean Sea that bathe us.

From this first experience, 4380 partners supported an immediate 4370 activity and already came out lines of service for our next meeting.

To paraphrase a beautiful poem, “love and friendship are simple, and simple things are eaten up by time”, so to dedicate the most valuable thing we can give to ourselves and others, to set aside time for good moments of friendship. It will always be more memorable, what we felt than what we did. We are confident that Rotary groups are the best way to strengthen and grow membership.

REFLECTIONS ON THE IYFR ANNUAL GENERAL MEETING IN SINGAPORE

By Jonathan Diaz, Fleet Commodore, Genuine Service President's -Sea Philippine Fleet

May 29-30, 2024
The rhythmic waves against our yacht’s hull create a soothing backdrop as I reflect on the whirlwind of events from the past two days. The International Yachting Fellowship of Rotarians (IYFR) AGM 2024 has just concluded in Singapore, leaving me with a wealth of experiences and insights.

May 29 Day One: Anticipation and Connections
Yesterday, Rotarians/IYforians from around the world gathered at the American Club in Singapore. The venue, blending colonial architecture with modern amenities, set the stage for our international meeting. Conversations buzzed in multiple languages as we exchanged handshakes, smiles, and business cards. The enthusiasm of the Philippine Fleets under the leadership of Regional Commodore Al Supangan stood out in terms of attendance, and discussions ranged from local sailing conditions to global Rotary initiatives.

Formal Proceedings and Heartfelt Moments
The General meeting and Area reporting were informative and they reminded us of our organization’s impact. It was presided by A3C Edmond Aguilar together with IPIC David Hansen, IRC Paul Yiu and PIC Jesus Avecilla. An inspirational message was delivered by the World Ambassador PRIP Gary Huang while IC Angie Mesistrano gave her message thru a video chat. The highlight came during the gala dinner – an elegant affair with nautical-themed centerpieces. Over exquisite cuisine, I engaged in fascinating conversations with Rotarians/IYforians from Taiwan, Ukraine, Singapore, Japan, Spain, USA, Canada, Australia, Africa, Netherlands and New Zealand. The cultural exchange was as rich as the food itself. A Certificate/Badge of Attendance for AGM ‘24 was also awarded to all participants.

May 30 Day Two: Sun, Sea, and Unexpected Fun

Today’s cruise around Sentosa Cove provided a contrast to the formality. Laughter and chatter filled the sun-drenched deck as we sailed past luxury residences and marinas. Snacks and drinks kept spirits high, and my wife Cecile even led an impromptu TikTok dance sessions – a surprising twist!

Looking Ahead

Discussions sparked ideas for joint projects and initiatives, I felt the unifying force of our shared passions. As we prepare to bid farewell to Singapore, I’m grateful for this experience.

The IYFR isn’t just about yachting; it’s about bridging cultures and making a difference. These memories will fuel my enthusiasm until next year’s meeting.

IYFR Kobe JAPAN Fleet 30th Anniversary Party was held on April 25, 2024.

The Kobe Fleet started in 1994 with 51 charter members. Over the past 30 years, the number of members has increased and decreased, and now there are 71 members in the fleet.

The reception and welcome drink started at 5:00 p.m., and the weather was fine.

The ceremony started at 6:00 p.m. with opening remarks by Executive Committee Chair Miki. Committee Chair Miki also presented a congratulatory address entrusted to him by RI President Gordon R. McInerney.

A reception followed, which was started with a greeting and toast from Past Commodore Takakimi Mihara, a charter member of the committee.

Greetings were given by the participants from Osaka Bay Fleet, Kyoto Lake Biwa Fleet, Setouchi-Shimanami Fleet, and Tokyo Fleet.

The time passed quickly and happily. A total of 63 people (including 18 from other fleets) participated in the event. We were able to hold a grand and enjoyable event.

Up to the Inauguration of the IYFR Kobe Fleet 1989 Masahiro Kamimura (RC Himeji Chuo) and Akira Miki (RC Himeji) joined IYFR at the Seoul International Convention.

1990 At the IYFR AGM at the Portland Convention, Mr. Kamimura was invited to create an IYFR fleet in Japan. After returning to Japan, he had a talk with Mr. Miki, who was a member of IYFR, and started groping in the dark for the establishment of the fleet.

After that, he corresponded with A.C. Mitchell (1991-93), IYFR International Commodore, several times and started preparations for the establishment of the IYFR fleet.

On February 26, 1994, IYFR Kobe Fleet Japan’s inaugural general meeting was held at Kobe Suma Yacht Club.

1994 Governor Kiyoshi Ishii and Governor-elect Tadashi Keima received their certificates and brought them back from the Taipei International Convention held June 12-15 (1993-95 International Commodore Jack C.L. Keyzer). June 18, 1994 Certificate Conveyance Ceremony held.

Members of IYFR Philippines gathered at the clubhouse of the Rotary Club of Malabon on May 2, 2024 for a dinner fellowship. May 2 is a special day for the officers and members of IYFR Philippines as it is the birthday of Regional Commodore Arturo “Al” Supangan.

The club members of the Rotary Club of Malabon as well as several Rotary Club officers of RI District 3800 were present to fellowship with IYFR members. It was a night of celebrations as there were quite a number of members who also celebrated their birthdays on the month of May.

A short formal meeting was preceded by fellowship that highlighted the birthday celebration of RC Al Supangan who shared birthday cakes to the May birthday celebrants. Rotarian guests enjoyed the camaraderie shared with IYFR members which made inviting them to join IYFR much easier.

PHILIPPINE FLEET BAY CRUISE IN SINGAPORE

By Regional Commodore Arturo “AL” T. Supangan II

The Rotary International Convention 2024 was held in Singapore, where Rotarians around the world gathered and celebrated the works of service that create lasting change. This convention created unforgettable moments and enduring relationships to the International Yachting Fellowship of Rotarians (IYFR) Mariners.

Group photos of early birds at Sentosa Jetty Port.
